EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two shortwave disturbances associated with a broad upper level trough lifted across the Four Corners area. The subsequent ample moisture and enhanced orographic lift contributed to heavy snow across the La Sal and Abajo Mountains.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The La Sal Mountains received 25 to 30 inches of snow by the end of the event with 6 to 13 inches estimated over the Abajo Mountains.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An average of 5 to 10 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts up to 14 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Three snowmobilers near Circleville Mountain triggered an avalanche, and one of them was killed in the accident.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three snowmobilers near Circleville Mountain triggered an 800-foot-wide avalanche, and two snowmobilers were caught, while the third made it to the bottom unharmed. One snowmobiler caught in the avalanche was able to dig out, and another was fully buried under 1-2 feet of snow. A search and rescue team was dispatched late in the afternoon, but was unable to continue operations overnight.  The buried snowmobiler was found the following morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Three snowmobilers near Chalk Creek triggered an avalanche and one of them was killed in the accident.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three snowmobilers were riding near Chalk Creek when one of them triggered a 1000- foot-wide avalanche and was buried in the snow. The other two snowmobilers immediately began search and rescue operations and located the buried snowmobiler after approximately 30 minutes. The buried snowmobiler was dug out of the snow and was found breathless, CPR was initiated and life flight helicopters were dispatched to the scene and arrived within 40 minutes. The snowmobiler was taken to Evanston Hospital and pronounced dead due to traumatic injury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Utah's first significant winter storm of 2019 entered the state on January 6 and brought heavy snowfall to both valley and mountain locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The unsettled, progressive weather pattern continued across eastern Utah during the month of March as numerous disturbances moved through. As a result of the wetter than normal conditions the state of the drought improved across the region, most notably in the Four Corners which finally eradicated the remaining severe (D2) drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The remaining severe (D2) drought conditions along the southern tier of the forecast zone improved to the moderate (D1) category by the end of the month. Elsewhere, the moderate conditions improved to the abnormally dry (D0) category.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep Pacific trough infiltrated the western U.S. and pushed across the Great Basin. A quick moving shortwave trough was ejected out ahead of the main system and into eastern Utah and the resulting combination of ample moisture, orographic lift, and additional upper level support from a 90 to 100 knot jet led to widespread precipitation across the region. Southwesterly flow out ahead of the system allowed snow to start over the La Sal and Abajo Mountains several hours earlier than further north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 6 inches of snow fell across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A group of snowmobilers recreating in the Dark Canyon Basin in the La Sal Mountains triggered a massive avalanche.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of January 25, a snowmobiler was buried in an avalanche in Dark Canyon, in the La Sal Mountains of southeastern Utah. His party attempted a rescue but they were unable to locate him and night was falling. Plans were made for search and rescue efforts to resume the following day. On Saturday, January 26, members from San Juan and Grand County Search and Rescues, Utah Department of Public Safety, and professional avalanche rescue dogs and handlers from Wasatch Backcountry Rescue, Alta, and Park City Ski areas conducted recovery efforts. The body was recovered late in the afternoon.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations ranged from 5 to 10 inches with 13.5 inches measured at the Kings Cabin SNOTEL. Peak winds hit 36 mph with blowing and drifting snow across Utah State Highway 191.

CZ_NAME: GREAT SALT LAKE DESERT AND MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another powerful storm brought snow and high winds across northern and central Utah. Overnight, a lake effect snow band brought significant snowfall to the Salt Lake Valley. This snow contained a high water content, taking tree branches down throughout the Salt Lake Valley, leading to power outages, and creating difficult travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The White Sage sensor recorded a peak wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two very moist and moderately warm Pacific storms pushed across eastern Utah. The second storm had abnormally high moisture content as it was accompanied by an atmospheric river. Favorable southwesterly flow allowed the southern mountains to receive significant snow by the end of the event. The excessive amount of moisture paired with the mild origins of the system resulted in low snow ratios which produced wet, heavy snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 5 to 10 inches of snow fell across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms producing heavy rain moved over active burn scars in central Utah, producing debris flows that impacted area roadways and resulted in water rescues.

The Utah Highway Patrol reported mud, rock, and other debris that was 2 feet thick across the roadway. U.S. Highway 89 was closed from the time of the event until 8:58 pm on July 28, 2019, as highway crews cleaned up and repaired the damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two skiers near Electric Lake triggered an avalanche and one of them was killed in the accident.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two skiers were skiing near Electric Lake in the late afternoon of January 18. A 500 feet wide avalanche was triggered and one skier was caught. The second skier made it to the bottom of the slope and discovered the avalanche once at the bottom of the slope. The skier searched through the avalanche debris, but was unable to find the trapped skier. Emery and Sanpete County search and rescue crews discovered the trapped skier approximately 16 hours later under approximately 3 feet of snow.  The skier was pronounced dead at the scene.
